data.content: I see the tombstone has .NET runtime frames from three native libraries with different Build IDs. Let me try to fetch debug symbols from Microsoft's symbol server and symbolicate them.

arguments.command: which llvm-symbolizer addr2line llvm-addr2line 2>/dev/null; echo "---"; which dotnet dotnet-symbol 2>/dev/null; echo "---"; dpkg -l | grep -i llvm 2>/dev/null | head -5

data.reasoningText:
The user wants me to symbolicate the .NET runtime frames. I can see several libraries in the backtrace:

1. `libmonosgen-2.0.so` (BuildId: 1eb39fc72918c7c6c0c610b79eb3d3d47b2f81be) - frames #01, #02, #03, #06
2. `libSystem.Native.so` (BuildId: 7a3b5c9d1e4f6082a3c5d7e9f1b2d4a6c8e0f213) - frames #04, #07


3. `libSystem.Globalization.Native.so` (BuildId: 2f4a6b8c0d2e4f6a8c0e2f4a6b8c0d2e4f6a8c0e) - frame #05

To resolve these addresses to actual function names, I'll need to locate the debug symbols for each library using their Build IDs and then use tools like `addr2line` or `llvm-symbolizer` to map the addresses. Microsoft publishes symbol packages for .NET runtime libraries that I can try to download. I should try the `dotnet-symbol` tool first if it's available, or fall back to manually downloading from Microsoft's symbol server. Looking at the build IDs I have for these native libraries, I can use those to locate the correct symbol files. I'll check what symbolication tools are available and attempt to resolve these symbols using the Microsoft symbol server.

arguments.command: which llvm-symbolizer addr2line llvm-addr2line 2>/dev/null; echo "---"; which dotnet dotnet-symbol 2>/dev/null; echo "---"; dpkg -l | grep -i llvm 2>/dev/null | head -5

toolRequests.name: bash

arguments.command:
mkdir -p /tmp/symbols && cd /tmp/symbols

# Try downloading debug symbols from Microsoft symbol server for each library
# Pattern: https://msdl.microsoft.com/download/symbols/<filename>/elf-buildid-<buildid>/<filename>

# libmonosgen-2.0.so (BuildId: 1eb39fc72918c7c6c0c610b79eb3d3d47b2f81be)
echo "=== Trying libmonosgen-2.0.so ==="
curl -sSL -o libmonosgen-2.0.so --fail \
  "https://msdl.microsoft.com/download/symbols/libmonosgen-2.0.so/elf-buildid-1eb39fc72918c7c6c0c610b79eb3d3d47b2f81be/libmonosgen-2.0.so" 2>&1 && echo "OK" || echo "FAIL"

curl -sSL -o libmonosgen-2.0.so.dbg --fail \
  "https://msdl.microsoft.com/download/symbols/_.debug/elf-buildid-sym-1eb39fc72918c7c6c0c610b79eb3d3d47b2f81be/_.debug" 2>&1 && echo "DBG OK" || echo "DBG FAIL"

# libSystem.Native.so (BuildId: 7a3b5c9d1e4f6082a3c5d7e9f1b2d4a6c8e0f213)
echo "=== Trying libSystem.Native.so ==="
curl -sSL -o libSystem.Native.so --fail \
  "https://msdl.microsoft.com/download/symbols/libSystem.Native.so/elf-buildid-7a3b5c9d1e4f6082a3c5d7e9f1b2d4a6c8e0f213/libSystem.Native.so" 2>&1 && echo "OK" || echo "FAIL"

curl -sSL -o libSystem.Native.so.dbg --fail \
  "https://msdl.microsoft.com/download/symbols/_.debug/elf-buildid-sym-7a3b5c9d1e4f6082a3c5d7e9f1b2d4a6c8e0f213/_.debug" 2>&1 && echo "DBG OK" || echo "DBG FAIL"

# libSystem.Globalization.Native.so (BuildId: 2f4a6b8c0d2e4f6a8c0e2f4a6b8c0d2e4f6a8c0e)
echo "=== Trying libSystem.Globalization.Native.so ==="
curl -sSL -o libSystem.Globalization.Native.so --fail \
  "https://msdl.microsoft.com/download/symbols/libSystem.Globalization.Native.so/elf-buildid-2f4a6b8c0d2e4f6a8c0e2f4a6b8c0d2e4f6a8c0e/libSystem.Globalization.Native.so" 2>&1 && echo "OK" || echo "FAIL"

curl -sSL -o libSystem.Globalization.Native.so.dbg --fail \
  "https://msdl.microsoft.com/download/symbols/_.debug/elf-buildid-sym-2f4a6b8c0d2e4f6a8c0e2f4a6b8c0d2e4f6a8c0e/_.debug" 2>&1 && echo "DBG OK" || echo "DBG FAIL"
arguments.description: Download symbols from Microsoft symbol server

toolRequests.intentionSummary: Download symbols from Microsoft symbol server

data.reasoningText:
We have `addr2line`, `dotnet-symbol`, and `dotnet` available. Let me try using `dotnet-symbol` to download the symbols for these libraries. But first, I need to note that `addr2line` is for the host architecture (amd64), while the libraries are arm64. So I'd need a cross-architecture addr2line, or use llvm tools which are architecture-independent.

Let me try using `dotnet-symbol` to download symbols using the build IDs. Actually, `dotnet-symbol` needs the actual binary or a path. Let me think about this differently.

The approach should be:


1. Try to download the debug symbols from Microsoft's symbol server using the build IDs
2. Use llvm-symbolizer (architecture-independent) to resolve addresses

For ELF files on the Microsoft symbol server, the URL format is `https://msdl.microsoft.com/download/symbols/<filename>/elf-buildid-sym-<buildid>/<filename>`. Let me try that for each library, though these build IDs might be fake since this is a sample file. Actually, `dotnet-symbol` might work better if I can pass it the right parameters.

I'm realizing I might need the actual binaries themselves to do symbolication, not just the symbols, or I might need to try a different approach altogether since we don't have the original binaries available.

Let me reconsider the symbol server URLs for ELF files—Microsoft's format uses the build ID in the path, so I'll try a few different URL patterns to see which one actually returns results. Since I can't directly access the binaries, I'll try hitting the Microsoft symbol server with different URL patterns for ELF files with build IDs. The standard paths are either the binary itself or the separate debug symbols, so I'll test both formats against each library's build ID.

data.content: Good — both files are valid ARM64 ELF with matching Build IDs. Now let me symbolicate.

arguments.command:
cd /tmp/sym_work

# Symbolicate libmonosgen-2.0.so frames (#01, #02, #03, #06)
# Using the debug file which has full symbols
for addr in 0x222098 0x222234 0x19bac0 0x19bc14; do
  echo "=== $addr ==="
  llvm-symbolizer-18 --obj=libmonosgen-2.0.so.dbg --output-style=GNU -f -C "$addr" 2>&1
  echo ""
done

- #05 pc 0x3b20 (libSystem.Globalization.Native.so)
